questions.question_text: Who completes the Louisiana property disclosure document?
distractors: The listing broker on behalf of the seller
A licensed home inspector
The buyer's agent after the inspection
questions.explanation: Louisiana law requires the seller to complete the LREC-prescribed property disclosure document in good faith. The broker does not complete it on the seller's behalf, and it is not a substitute for a professional home inspection.

questions.question_text: When must the property disclosure document be delivered to the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: Before or at the time the buyer makes an offer
distractors: Within 10 days after the offer is accepted
At closing only if the buyer requests it
After the home inspection is completed
questions.explanation: The property disclosure must be delivered to the buyer before or at the time an offer is made. This timing ensures the buyer has material information about the property's condition before committing to the transaction.

questions.question_text: A seller delivers the property disclosure document two days after the buyer submits an offer. What right does the buyer have?
questions.correct_answer: A 72-hour right to withdraw the offer
distractors: The right to void the contract at any time
A 30-day rescission period from closing
No additional rights beyond the contract terms
questions.explanation: When the disclosure is delivered late (after the offer), the buyer receives a 72-hour withdrawal right, excluding weekends and holidays. This protects the buyer who made an offer without the benefit of the disclosure information.

questions.question_text: The 72-hour buyer withdrawal period for late property disclosure excludes which days?
distractors: Only federal holidays recognized by the state
No days are excluded from the count
Banking holidays and Mardi Gras only
questions.explanation: When calculating the 72-hour withdrawal period for late delivery of the property disclosure, weekends and legal holidays are excluded. This ensures the buyer has a meaningful period of actual business days to review the disclosure and decide whether to withdraw.

questions.question_text: A seller intentionally omits a known foundation defect from the property disclosure form. What is the most likely legal exposure?
distractors: No liability if the buyer waived inspection
A small fine payable to the LREC only
Liability only if the defect is discovered at closing
questions.explanation: The seller must complete the disclosure in good faith. Intentionally concealing a known defect constitutes fraud or misrepresentation, exposing the seller to civil liability for damages. A buyer's waiver of inspection does not shield a seller who commits fraud.

questions.question_text: Which type of listing gives one broker the exclusive right to a commission regardless of who sells the property?
distractors: Open listing agreement with the seller
Exclusive agency listing agreement
Net listing agreement with the seller
questions.explanation: An exclusive right to sell listing guarantees the broker a commission no matter who procures the buyer, including the seller. This differs from an exclusive agency listing, where the seller retains the right to sell independently without owing a commission.

questions.question_text: Under Louisiana law, a valid listing agreement must be which of the following?
questions.correct_answer: Written, signed by all owners, and for a defined period
distractors: Oral, if confirmed by two witnesses
Signed by at least one co-owner of the property
Open-ended until the property sells
questions.explanation: R.S. 37:1431(16) defines a listing agreement as a written document signed by all owners of the real estate (or their authorized attorney in fact) authorizing a broker to offer the property on specified terms for a defined period of time, and provides that it is valid only if signed by all owners. R.S. 37:1449(C) additionally requires written agreements for the sale or management of real estate to specify a definite expiration date not subject to qualifying terms or conditions.

questions.question_text: A broker has a buyer sign a purchase agreement but fails to provide the buyer with a copy. Has the broker violated Louisiana law?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, copies must be delivered to all signers
distractors: No, copies are only required at closing
No, the buyer can request a copy later
Only if the buyer is unrepresented
questions.explanation: Louisiana requires brokers to deliver copies of all signed documents to all parties who signed them. Failing to provide a copy of the purchase agreement to the buyer is a violation regardless of whether the buyer requests one.

questions.question_text: How does an exclusive agency listing differ from an exclusive right to sell listing?
questions.correct_answer: The seller may sell independently without commission
distractors: Only one type requires a written agreement
The exclusive agency has no expiration date
The broker earns a higher commission rate
questions.explanation: Under an exclusive agency listing, one broker is the exclusive agent, but the seller retains the right to find a buyer independently without owing a commission. Under an exclusive right to sell, the broker earns a commission regardless of who finds the buyer.

questions.question_text: A listing agreement does not include an expiration date. What is the legal effect in Louisiana?
questions.correct_answer: It violates the required definite expiration date rule
distractors: It automatically expires after 90 days
It remains in effect until the property sells
LREC assigns a standard 6-month duration
questions.explanation: R.S. 37:1449(C) requires that written agreements for the sale or management of real estate specify a definite expiration date that is not subject to qualifying terms or conditions, and R.S. 37:1431(16) defines a listing agreement as covering a defined period of time. An agreement without an expiration date violates these requirements and exposes the licensee to discipline. No statute or LREC rule supplies an automatic default duration.

questions.question_text: What did Act 690 (2024) require for buyer representation in Louisiana?
questions.correct_answer: Written buyer agreements for 1-4 unit purchases
distractors: Verbal buyer agreements for all property types
Written agreements only for commercial sales
Mandatory buyer agency for all transactions
questions.explanation: Act 690, effective in 2024, requires written buyer representation agreements specifically for purchases of 1-4 unit residential properties. This aligns Louisiana with industry-wide changes in buyer representation practices.

questions.question_text: Act 690 requires a written buyer agreement for which property types?
distractors: All property types including commercial
Only single-family detached homes
Properties valued at $250,000 or more
questions.explanation: Act 690 applies specifically to purchases of residential properties containing 1 to 4 dwelling units. Commercial properties and larger multifamily buildings are not covered by this particular requirement. There is no value threshold.

questions.question_text: A buyer wants to tour a 3-unit residential property with an agent but has not signed a buyer agreement. Under Act 690, what should the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Have the buyer sign a written agreement first
distractors: Proceed without an agreement for showings only
Obtain verbal consent and document it later
Refer the buyer to the listing agent instead
questions.explanation: Act 690 (R.S. 37:1448.4) requires a written buyer agreement signed by the broker and buyer whenever a buyer uses a licensee's services toward purchasing a home of one to four units; a verbal arrangement does not satisfy the statute. Under the nationwide NAR-settlement practice changes that Act 690 accompanies, the agreement should be executed before the agent tours properties with the buyer.

questions.question_text: Who are the parties to a buyer representation agreement under Act 690?
distractors: The salesperson and the buyer
The buyer, seller, and broker jointly
The buyer and the LREC
questions.explanation: Under Act 690, the written buyer representation agreement is between the broker and the buyer. Although a salesperson may work directly with the buyer, the agreement is with the broker, consistent with Louisiana's requirement that all agency relationships run through the broker.

questions.question_text: A broker represents a buyer purchasing a 6-unit apartment building. Does Act 690 require a written buyer agreement for this transaction?
questions.correct_answer: No, Act 690 applies only to 1-4 unit properties
distractors: Yes, all residential purchases require one
Yes, if the purchase price exceeds $500,000
Only if the buyer requests representation
questions.explanation: Act 690 specifically applies to residential properties of 1 to 4 units. A 6-unit apartment building falls outside the scope of this requirement. However, a broker may still choose to use a written buyer agreement as best practice even when not legally mandated.
